retrieved_docs.title: Influenza Vaccine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Differently combined HA and NA antigens are seen in influenza A, which undergo antigenic drifts and shifts resulting in antigenic variation and, thereby, the necessity for vaccine strain types to vary accordingly. Antigenic drifts are genetic changes occurring in the virus due to various actions of polymerases leading to gradual antigenic changes in both HA and NA, producing new variant strains. An antigenic shift occurs when the currently circulating virus disappears and is replaced by a new subtype with novel glycoproteins, to which antibodies against the previously circulating subtype do not cross-react. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Antigen: A molecule that is recognized by a specific antibody or T-cell receptor (TCR)

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Memory B cells: Long-lasting B-cells that are responsive to one particular antigen and become activated with re-exposure to the same antigen

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: MHC II: Found on antigen-presenting cells that interact with CD4 T cells; responsible for presenting exogenous antigens

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Plasma cells : Permanently differentiated B-cells that secrete antibodies

retrieved_docs.title: Allergy Immunotherapy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Allergy immunotherapy (AIT),  also referred to as allergen desensitization or hypo-sensitization was first introduced by Leonard Noon and John Freeman in 1911, they proposed that people with hay fever were sensitive to grass pollen toxins. [1] Noon is credited for developing a process involved in the extraction of timothy pollen in distilled water and then boiling it to create an extract. [1] This extract was then injected in increasing doses to alleviate symptoms in patients.  This concept is widely in use today with a modified approach. Currently, the term immunotherapy is used to describe all methods to overcome abnormal immune responses with induction of clonal deletion, anergy, immune tolerance, and immune deviation. [2] However, the term desensitization is "a method, to develop a temporary state of tolerance to an agent responsible for an allergic or hypersensitivity reaction." [3] Furthermore, immunotherapy is a disease-modifying treatment and the effects can last longer even after stopping the treatment, which then provides prophylactic effects. [3] [4] [5] [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Immunogen: Protein or carbohydrate that is recognized and sufficiently activates an immune response

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Major histocompatibility complex (MHC) I: Found on all nucleated cells, play a significant role in determining “self.” Responsible for presenting intracellular antigens to CD8 T cells

retrieved_docs.title: Oseltamivir -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Mechanism of Resistance: The mechanism of resistance of influenza viruses involves antigenic drift and shift in their surface glycoproteins. Antigenic drift is the process in which minor modifications occur in key viral epitopes through point mutations; antigenic shift results in a complete exchange of hemagglutinin (HA) or neuraminidase (NA) genes. Antigenic shift emerges mainly in influenza A viruses due to their vast animal reservoirs. Antigenic shift is a process frequently associated with the outbreak of pandemics. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding How Monoclonal Antibodies Work -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The antibodies generated by this immune response are proteins that have high specificity and affinity for the antigen/ molecule they were generated against. Kohler and Milstein utilized these principles to conceive what came to be called a "hybridoma" (a fusion cell composed of myeloma cells and splenic B lymphocytes, both murine in origin). [1] [2] [4] These hybrid cells allowed Kohler and Milstein to reliably produce a single antibody clone in large volumes and with a pre-selected specificity, which became known as mAbs. The initial biotechnology of primitive mAbs became quickly problematic when the murine origin of such proteins caused them to become immunogenic and non-sustainable for long-term therapy due to the development of human anti-murine antibodies (HAMA). [4] [5] The increased clearance due to HAMA generation was also accompanied by eventual Immunoglobulin E (IgE) development and anaphylactic reactions on subsequent administrations. [1] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Adjuvant: Prolongs the presence of antigen in tissue and enhances the immune response to an antigen; used in acquired or artificial immunization (vaccinations)

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: TH2: The response that occurs in the absence of IL-12 and IFN-gamma; promotes systemic antibody driven response; releases IL-4, IL-5, IL-6, IL-10 cytokines

retrieved_docs.title: Allergy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The most common triggers of anaphylaxis include foods, medications, insect stings, and allergen immunotherapies. Any substance that can trigger the degranulation of mast cells or basophils can induce anaphylaxis. The definition of allergy and anaphylaxis has varied in the past; in 2005, an interprofessional group of clinical experts was formed to improve recognition and designate criteria for the diagnosis of anaphylaxis. The incidence of anaphylaxis varies between 0.5 to 2% in the general population, and the rate has been increasing. The lifetime prevalence is 1.6%. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Allergy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: There is a concept of a direct immune response to medications and drugs or even biologic therapies that use proteins to stimulate a response. This concept involves the pharmacological interaction of drugs with immune receptors known as the p-i concept). The theory is that certain drugs in their naive state without processing or metabolism can stimulate T cells through T cell receptors (TCRs) or HLA molecules that are not their primary therapeutic targets. This stimulation is called “off-target” activity and explains genetic propensity through TCRs and HLA of individuals. A specific example is drugs that produce a drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms/drug-induced hypersensitivity syndrome. (DRESS/DiHS) such as flucloxacillin and sulfamethoxazole). The mechanisms of p-i TCR and p-i HLA are different, but the concept is direct off-target induction of the immune system is the concept. In the p-i TCR model, a drug interacts with a TCR. Then a second interaction between the TCR and HLA molecules on APCs is required to stimulate a cellular proliferation, cytokine production, and cytotoxicity by the drug stimulated T Cells. Sulfamethoxazole, as an example, is a common public epitope, but another factor is required to induce it, such as a generalized viral infection. [12] [13] [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Cellular Level -- Relevant Terms and Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Dendritic cells (antigen-presenting cells): Facilitate activation of an antigen-specific response by the innate system; present antigens via major histone complexes to activate CD8 and CD4 T cells

Overall, the integrated framework unifies diverse drug classes through a structural‑analogue taxonomy, connects them to a diagnostic radiopharmaceutical, and provides explicit hypersensitivity contraindications, thereby supporting consistent clinical documentation, safe prescribing and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1591
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease encompasses human vaccination as a specific form of immunization, indicating that vaccines administered to humans can be used to prevent or modulate autoimmune conditions. Human vaccination is situated within the broader category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, reflecting its role as a preventive intervention designed to protect individuals from infectious agents. Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations functions as the overarching classification that groups together all preventive vaccine strategies, including human vaccination, and thereby provides the hierarchical framework linking general preventive immunization concepts to the more specific application of immunization for autoimmune disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1763
retrieved_docs.content: supports clinicians, researchers, and regulators in recognizing authorized drugs, understanding their mechanisms and manufacturing routes, and applying consistent safety and efficacy criteria in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease includes human vaccination as a subordinate concept, indicating that human vaccination is regarded as a specific form of immunization employed to prevent or modulate autoimmune disease in humans.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2212

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2099
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations are identified as a category that classifies itself, meaning the concept of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations is used to define and encompass the same set of preventive immunizations and inoculation practices. This self‑referential classification underscores that the term represents a distinct group of preventive interventions within medical terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1314

Collectively, these diverse entities serve as important qualifiers for describing and investigating physiological mechanisms, disease processes, and therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 955
retrieved_docs.content: Arthritis, acquired hemolytic anemia, cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia, coronary artery inflammation, and a form of hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity are all classified within the “Immune disorders/Autoimmune (SMQ)” grouping. This categorization reflects that these conditions are relevant to immune‑mediated disease processes and are monitored together in pharmacovigilance and clinical practice. While most of the listed disorders are driven by autoimmune mechanisms, the non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia is included in the same SMQ to ensure comprehensive safety surveillance of all hemolytic events that may arise in the context of immune system modulation. Recognizing the shared immunological context of these entities aids clinicians in identifying potential autoimmune contributions, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing therapeutic decisions across a spectrum of inflammatory and hematologic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: antiviral therapy (e.g., telaprevir). Together, these entities illustrate a comprehensive therapeutic landscape that integrates hierarchical classification, mechanistic specificity, drug‑interaction considerations, diagnostic imaging agents, and a spectrum of antiviral modalities—from nucleoside analogues and entry inhibitors to monoclonal antibodies—each with defined indications and roles essential for optimized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 39
retrieved_docs.content: antibodies, and membrane‑transport modulators—through shared mechanisms, therapeutic uses, contraindications, and ATC classifications, providing a structured reference for safe application, dosage determination, and patient‑centered care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2893
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an “Allowed qualifier” is applied to arboviruses, bacterial vaccines, the drug desonid, and the term metabolismus, indicating that these entities can be classified or described using specific qualifying criteria. Metabolismus itself is linked to the monoclonal antibody omalizumab, reflecting a relationship between metabolic processes and this targeted therapy, and it is also associated with سعفة الرأس (head lice), highlighting the relevance of metabolic considerations in the context of parasitic infestations. Together, these connections illustrate how qualifying descriptors are used across diverse areas such as viral infections, immunizations, pharmacology, metabolic pathways, biologic treatments, and parasitology, underscoring the integrated nature of medical classification and patient‑care considerations.

Together, these interrelationships clarify how drug classes, individual agents, chemical families, therapeutic uses, mechanisms of action, contraindications and adverse‑effect profiles are organized, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies, anticipating co‑occurring risks and monitoring relevant clinical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.
